Q1: We need all four categories assessed — empirical claims with evidence, value-laden claims, rhetorical moves, and debate-specific structures like DAs, CPs, and kritiks. Pure deductive validity is actually the least central; most high school debate arguments are inductive or evidentiary. The biggest weight is on evidence quality and how well claims are warranted, followed by relevance/responsiveness to the opponent's case.
Q2: We want separate sub-scores (logic, evidence, relevance, persuasiveness) plus qualitative coaching feedback explaining weaknesses — a single composite score isn't actionable for student improvement. We also want it to handle pairwise comparison when students practice rebuttals. 'Strong' is defined by a mix of mainstream judging norms (NSDA, TOC circuit paradigms) and our coaching staff's rubrics, not a purely platform-internal standard.
Q3: It needs to adapt by format and ideally by circuit. Our students compete in PF, LD, and policy across both local and national circuits, and what wins in a lay PF round in Texas is very different from a tech-heavy LD round at a TOC bid tournament. At minimum we'd want format-level adaptation; circuit-level adaptation would be a strong plus.
Q4: Yes — evaluating evidence credibility and recency matters a lot, since 'card quality' is central to competitive debate and we want to flag cherry-picked, outdated, or miscontextualized evidence. For empirically false but internally consistent arguments, we'd want the system to note the factual problem while still scoring the logical structure separately, since debaters need feedback on both dimensions.
